items.q: Is language change a sign of decline?
items.correct: No — modern linguistics treats change as natural and continuous, not as decay. Every language has always been changing and will continue to.
items.misconception: Language change shows that standards are falling and English is being ruined.
items.random_wrong: Language change only happens in informal speech, not in writing.
items.why: The 'decline' view is prescriptivist folk-belief. Linguists observe that what one generation laments as 'wrong' is often what previous generations also lamented and became standard — change is constant.

items.q: What is the telegraphic stage of child language development?
items.correct: Around age 2-3, children produce short utterances containing content words but omitting function words and inflections (e.g. 'mummy gone shop').
items.misconception: It is the stage when children first start using a telephone.
items.random_wrong: It is the stage where children memorise long stories.
items.why: The name comes from telegrams — which dropped articles and prepositions to save money. Students misread the term literally rather than understanding the analogy.

items.q: Are features of spoken language like 'um' and 'you know' signs of poor speech?
items.correct: No — they are functional discourse markers and planning devices that help interaction flow, manage turn-taking and signal speaker meaning.
items.misconception: Fillers and false starts show the speaker is uneducated.
items.random_wrong: Spoken language has no rules unlike written language.
items.why: Spoken features serve real purposes (hedging, turn-holding, processing time). Students apply written-language standards to speech, treating its natural features as failure rather than as features of a different mode.

items.q: David Crystal's position on internet English is that it is:
items.correct: A new third medium enriching English
items.why: It's A new third medium enriching English. “A symptom of decline” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The S-curve model of adoption was developed by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Chen. “Crystal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: David Crystal's 'Language and the Internet' was published in:
items.why: The real figure is 2001. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
